rSOfl, 


city of orange 
city attorney 
(714) 532-0351 
orange civic center • 300 east chapman ave 
October 24, 1980 
Fair ~olitical Practices Commission 
Conflicts of Interest Division 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, ~a. 95814 
Gentlemen: 
R.qUMt No .. 80 oofl 
I would like to request an opinion concerning an 
allegation that ~ayor Pro Tern non Smith has a conflict of 
interest which would prevent him from voting on the question 
of the conversion of certain rental apartment units to own-
your-own condominiums. 
Enclosed herein is a street map of the ~ity of Orange 
on which I have indicated the location of the apartments 
which have requested conversion to condominiums. 
mhese 
requests have been presented to the Planning ~om~ission and 
the City Council in the form of Tentative Tract Approval 
Applications. 
The first Tentative Tract ~o. 10867 consists 
of 84 units which is indicated on the enclosed map. 
~he 
second application is adjacent thereto and consists of 
~entative Tract No. 10866 containing 12 units. 
At the City Council meeting of Tuesday, October 14, 
1980, after public hearings before the ~lanning ~ommission 
and after several continued public hearings before the ~ity 
Council, one speaker, a tenant from a development other than 
the subject development seeking conversion to condominium 
ownership, spoke and alleged that ~ayor ~ro ~em Smith owned 
rental units in the City of Orange. 
Indeed, Mayor 'Oro mem 
Smith does own rental units in the City of Orange and he has 
clearly indicated the location of such rental units in Form 
721 which he has consistently filed each year with the City 
Clerk of the ~ity of Orange. 
I enclose a copy of his latest 
Form 721 filed with our City ~lerk. 
~oreover, I have indi-
cated on the enclosed map the location in the City of rental 
units owned by Mayor Pro Tern Smith. 
Most of these rental 
units are duplexes or triplexes. 
I believe he owns no more 
than 4 rental units located on a single parcel. 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
October 24, 1980 
Page two 
As a result of this allegation, Mayor Pro ~em Smith 
requested that I seek, on his behalf, an opinion from the 
Fair political Practices Commission concerning any conflict 
of interest which he might have in voting on the proposed 
conversion. 
As an abstract economic principle, it is true, 
as alleged by this non-resident tenant, that the greater the 
number of conversions, the less there exists a supply of 
rental housing in the City and, therefore, there may be some 
tendency to increase the value of such remaining rental 
units. 
This increase in the value of rental units assumes, 
of course, that the supply of rental units remains constant. 
No one knows when apartment building will begin again with 
the same activity that it exhibited in the early 1960's. 
As 
soon as the economy permits such apartment building, rental 
units will again be developed. 
At the hearing I pointed out that the tenant who was 
speaking had a real job on his hands to prove that it was 
"reasonably foreseeable" that the conversion would have a 
"material financial effect" on the real property rentals 
owned by Mayor Pro Tern Smith. 
Frankly, I did not hear any 
such evidence presented by this speaker at the hearing. 
In 
fact, you will notice that I am sending a copy of this 
letter to that speaker, as well as to the President of the 
tenants association in the subject conversion area to see if 
they have any such evidence as to such material effect. 
Also enclosed herein you will find a copy of the letter 
of June 3, 1980, from your agency to me concerning the 
conflict of interest question presented by Orange City 
Councilman Fred Barrera. 
You will notice that in that 
opinion you emphasized the importance of the phrases "reason-
ably foreseeable" that there would be a "material financial 
effect." 
Also, I enclose herein some statistics from the 
Planning Department of the City indicating the total number 
of rental units in the City, as well as some of the major 
rentals on the boundary of the City in other cities which 
are available for rental. 
The large industrial plants in 
the City are located on the west boundary near the Santa Ana 
River. 
This area is circled in yellow, cross-hatched in 
brown. 
~he employees of these plants might be expected to 
create a demand for rentals. 
Now, of course, it is true 
that here, as in many other areas of the state, the vacancy 
factor is very low. 
Most of the units are rented. 
Of 
course, these units that are already rented include those 
owned by Mayor Pro ~em Smith. 
~herefore, Mayor Pro ~em 
Smith does not need to worry about having enough applicants 
to rent his units regardless of any vote he would cast to 
approve this conversion since at the present time he appears 
to be able to obtain as many tenants as he needs to keep his 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
October 24, 1980 
Page three 
rentals occupied. 
You will notice that the number of rental 
units in the City, as indicated by the Planning Department 
statistics are considerable, perhaps 11,000 units. 
Note 
also, the statistical material of rental units in other 
cities. 
Anaheim in areas near the boundaries of the City of 
Orange has approximately 3,237 Multiple-Family units none of 
which have been converted to ownership. 
Santa Ana ha.s 
approximately 2,000 Hultiple-Family units in areas near the 
City of Orange of which 140 units have been converted to 
ownership. 
This is a total of more than 5,000 rental units 
in areas of nearby cities readily accessible to Orange. 
When you add Garden Grove's estimated 500 rental units in 
areas near the City of Orange, you have at least 16,000 or 
more rental units available to tenants. 
You will notice 
also that the number of units so far converted in the City 
is rather small in proportion to the total number of units 
that exist, perhaps only 1,000 units. 
The supply-demand 
situation at the present time favors landlords who rent 
units. 
If we desire to make more rental units available, it 
is clear that we will have to permit the private sector 
through free market incentives and profits to produce more 
rental units which people can rent if we are to solve the 
tenant demand. 
Neither have recent statewide threats of 
rent control been reassuring to developers of residential 
rental developments. 
I have indicated the location of a small private col-
lege in the City, Chapman College. 
The housing office 
indicates the school has about 1200 students. 
Six Hundred 
and fifty (650) of these students live on campus in dorms or 
college owned apartments. 
The dorms are not full but only 
about 85% to 90% occupied. 
There is a waiting list for 
college owned apartments because the rents are less then 
market. 
Of the 550 students who live off campus, probably 
half of these live at home and commute. 
Only 200 to 300 
students at most might seek to rent privately owned apart-
ments. 
Therefore, the student popUlation of the City does 
not appear to pose excessive ~emand for privately owned 
rental units. 
If the foregoing factors indicate a conflict of in-
terest on the part of councilmen owning rental units in the 
City, I don't see how anyone who is involved in owning land 
as a source of income in the City would ever be able to vote 
on matters involving land development or ownership. 
Remember too, that conversions of rental units to units 
separately owned creates the possibility of home ownership 
for a great number of people who cannot afford a single 
family house on a separate lot of land. 
Also, rentals 
located in the City are made available by the departure from 
such rentals by persons who purchase units so converted to 
ownership. 


Fair Political Practices Co~~ission 
October 24, 1980 
Page four 
I would appreciate your prompt attention to this issue 
if possible, inasmuch as the matter has been continued for 
an opinion by your body before the Council will act on this 
conversion request. 
We would like to again set this Matter 
for hearing in late November or early DeceMber. 
~aturally, 
the applicant for the conversion is anxious for a prompt 
answer to this question. 
I stand ready to answer any further inquiries which you 
may have and supply you with any information you need in 
order to answer the request by Mayor Pro ~em Smith for an 
opinion on this matter. 
FBR:cfk 
cc: 
City Council 
City Manager 
City Clerk 
Very truly yours, 
/-~ 
/:' 
f 
f // 'ii'-
,,,,~,,,r{..,,,,,,,'"C'"J 
-" 
Furman B. Roberts 
City Attorney 
Director of Planning & Development Services 
Mr. Robert Holland, Attorney at Law 
Mr. Jeff Burns 
Mr. Marvin Marple

FBR:cfk 
cc: 
City Council 
City Manager 
City Clerk
sections.conclusion: There is no hi[gh level of] conflict of interest in this matter. Mr. Smith's rental units are not directly affected by the pending conversions because his properties do not appear to have any material financial effect that is reasonably foreseeable from the conversion requests. The City should determine the standards or criteria for evaluating condominium conversion requests and apply them consistently.
sections.question_synthetic: Does Mayor Pro Tem Smith's ownership of rental units in the City of Orange create a conflict of interest that would disqualify him from voting on condominium conversion applications?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mayor Pro Tem Smith does not have a conflict of interest preventing him from voting on the condominium conversion applications because it is not reasonably foreseeable that the conversions would have a material financial effect on his rental properties.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document heavily degraded by OCR with significant text corruption throughout. FPPC response letter is largely illegible in middle sections. Extracted conclusion and question from more readable portions, but completeness uncertain. Contains both incoming request (October 24) and FPPC response (November 21) in single document. References to specific case precedent (Barrera opinion) present but text unclear.

CONCLUSION: There is no hi[gh level of] conflict of interest in this matter. The City should determine the standards or criteria for evaluating condominium conversion requests and apply them consistently.

Enclosed herein is a street map of the ~ity of Orange on which I have
embedding.summary: The City of Orange requested FPPC advice on whether Mayor Pro Tem Smith's ownership of rental units in the city created a conflict of interest regarding his potential vote on apartment-to-condominium conversion applications. The FPPC advised that no conflict exists because the financial impact on Smith's rental properties from the conversions is not reasonably foreseeable.
